name: API paradigms & realtime
description: REST, GraphQL, gRPC, SOAP/RPC and realtime transports — choosing and defending an API style. From the 'API paradigmák' prep doc.

topics.name: GraphQL
topics.description: Query language with a single endpoint, client-specified fields, one round trip.
topics.studyNotes:
GraphQL is a query language over a single endpoint: the CLIENT specifies exactly which fields it wants, and gets them in one round trip. That is the whole pitch — it solves REST's over-fetching (the endpoint returns more than needed) and under-fetching (one page needs three endpoints, three round trips, or an N+1 cascade of calls).

The decision frame: how varied and demanding are the clients' data needs? Many different clients (mobile, web, partners) each wanting different slices of a rich graph → GraphQL shines. A stable resource CRUD API with one predictable consumer → REST is simpler.

The costs to say out loud: server-side complexity (resolvers, schema), HTTP caching no longer free (everything is a POST to one endpoint — you need persisted queries or application-level caching), rate limiting harder (one query can be cheap or enormous — cost analysis needed), and the server-side N+1 problem: a naive resolver fetches each list item's relations one by one; the fix is batching with a DataLoader-style layer.

REST's strengths remain real: simplicity, cache-friendliness, universal tooling. The honest answer is per-situation, never "GraphQL is newer therefore better".

topics.name: gRPC
topics.description: Contract-first RPC over HTTP/2 with protobuf, streaming, strong typing.
topics.studyNotes:
gRPC is contract-first RPC over HTTP/2 with Protocol Buffers: a .proto file defines the strict contract, code generation produces typed client and server stubs, messages travel as compact binary. It is built for service-to-service calls: low latency, multiplexed HTTP/2 connections, and native streaming — server-side (a feed of updates), client-side (uploading chunks), and bidirectional (live sync).

When it wins: internal microservice communication where performance and a strict typed contract matter — a payment-service call generates its client from the proto and cannot silently drift from the contract.

The limitations that complete the answer: browsers cannot call gRPC natively (grpc-web plus a proxy is required), binary payloads are harder to debug than JSON (no curl-and-look), and external partners generally find REST/JSON friendlier. So the typical shape is REST at the edge for people and partners, gRPC inside between services.

topics.name: Realtime transports
topics.description: Polling, SSE, WebSocket and webhooks for pushing data to clients.
topics.studyNotes:
Four ways to get data to the other side, distinguished by direction and initiator:

- Polling — client pulls repeatedly. Simple, works everywhere, wasteful at frequency; fine for low-frequency checks.
- SSE (Server-Sent Events) — one-way server push over plain HTTP with automatic reconnection. Right for notifications, live feeds, dashboards.
- WebSocket — a persistent, bidirectional connection. Right when the client also sends continuously: chat, games, collaborative editing.
- Webhook — server-to-server callback: your system POSTs to a registered URL when an event happens. Right for third-party integrations; brings retries, signatures, idempotency and delivery logging as your responsibility.

The scaling note that marks a senior answer: persistent connections cost server resources and complicate load balancing — a WebSocket binds to an instance (stateful), so horizontal scaling needs a pub/sub backbone (Redis) behind the instances. SSE multiplexes over HTTP/2 and passes proxies more easily. Choose by direction of data flow first, operational cost second.

questions.title: REST vs GraphQL
questions.prompt: How would you decide between REST and GraphQL for a new API?
questions.answerStructureHint: Anchor in client needs; over/under-fetching and one round trip vs caching, complexity and versioning.

expectedPoints.label: Frames around client needs
expectedPoints.description: Decides based on how varied and demanding the clients' data needs are.

expectedPoints.label: GraphQL solves over/under-fetching
expectedPoints.description: Client picks exactly the fields it needs in one request; avoids many round trips.

expectedPoints.label: REST strengths and GraphQL costs
expectedPoints.description: REST is simple, cache-friendly (HTTP caching); GraphQL adds server complexity, caching and rate-limiting challenges.

followUps.prompt: GraphQL can trigger an N+1 problem on the server. What is it and how would you address it?

questions.title: When to use gRPC
questions.prompt: When would you choose gRPC over a REST or GraphQL API?
questions.answerStructureHint: Internal service-to-service, performance, streaming and contracts vs browser support and tooling.

expectedPoints.label: Service-to-service performance
expectedPoints.description: gRPC suits internal microservice calls: binary protobuf over HTTP/2, low latency, streaming.

expectedPoints.label: Contract-first with generated code
expectedPoints.description: Proto files define a strict contract and generate typed client/server stubs.

expectedPoints.label: Knows the browser/tooling limitation
expectedPoints.description: Not natively callable from browsers (needs grpc-web/proxy); harder to debug than JSON.

followUps.prompt: gRPC supports streaming. Give an example where server or bidirectional streaming is the right fit.

questions.title: Realtime: polling vs SSE vs WebSocket vs webhook
questions.prompt: Compare polling, server-sent events, WebSocket and webhooks. When does each fit?
questions.answerStructureHint: Direction, who initiates, connection cost, and the classic use cases for each.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes by direction and initiator
expectedPoints.description: Polling/SSE are client-pull/server-push one-way; WebSocket is bidirectional; webhooks are server-to-server callbacks.

expectedPoints.label: Right use case for each
expectedPoints.description: Polling for simple/low-frequency, SSE for notifications/feeds, WebSocket for chat/live collaboration, webhook for third-party events.

expectedPoints.label: Mentions connection cost / scaling
expectedPoints.description: Persistent connections (WebSocket) cost server resources and complicate load balancing.

followUps.prompt: A webhook delivery can fail. How do you make webhook consumption reliable?
